conjecture_title: Open problem (disjoint union of good trees)
paper_title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VII. Disjoint holes
summary: The general question of whether the disjoint union of two good trees is always good remains open as of 2026. Partial progress exists: arXiv:2202.10412 (submitted three days after the source paper) proves that if $H$ is a good forest then $H \cup P_4$ is also good, and the source paper itself notes that the disjoint union of two good forests is good whenever one is self-isolating. No paper resolving the full conjecture was found in the indexed literature.

since_posted.title: Polynomial bounds for chromatic number VI. Adding a four-vertex path
since_posted.authors: Maria Chudnovsky, Alex Scott, Paul Seymour, Sophie Spirkl
since_posted.claim: Proves that if $H$ is a good forest then the disjoint union of $H$ and $P_4$ is also good, establishing in particular that $2P_4$-free graphs are polynomially $\chi$-bounded, but falls short of proving the conjecture for arbitrary pairs of good trees.

internal_refs_verified.note: Confirmed relevant: proves H ∪ P₄ is good when H is a good forest; directly provides partial progress on the conjecture.
Not relevant: paper concerns suns in triangle-free graphs of large chromatic number, with no connection to disjoint unions of good trees.
Not relevant: paper is about clock-free graphs and treewidth obstructions; the attributed contribution text about proving the conjecture in full is a false positive from the extraction pipeline.

search_queries: disjoint union good trees polynomially chi-bounded Chudnovsky Scott Seymour Spirkl 2202.09118
polynomially chi-bounded disjoint union good forest self-isolating Chudnovsky Spirkl 2023 2024 2025
"disjoint union" "good" trees "chi-bounded" OR "chromatic" forest conjecture arXiv 2024 2025 Spirkl Scott
notes: arXiv:2311.05719 appears three times in the internal references with a contribution text that describes a completely different conjecture (clock-free graphs, treewidth) — likely a fuzzy-matching false positive. arXiv:2202.10412 is a companion paper in the same 'Polynomial bounds' series submitted three days after the source paper by the same four authors; its H ∪ P₄ result is the strongest known partial answer to the open problem.
